title: Abutilon sandwicense
intro: Abutilon sandwicense, also known as Ma'o hau hele, is a perennial shrub native to Hawaii, belonging to the Malvaceae family. It has been traditionally used in Hawaiian medicine for its health benefits, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, making it effective in treating various ailments such as fever, sore eyes, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Abutilon sandwicense include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional Hawaiian medicine, Abutilon sandwicense is often prepared as a tea or infusion for treating various health issues, and its leaves are also used to make a topical salve or ointment.
taxonomy: Abutilon sandwicense is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Malvaceae, commonly known as the hibiscus or mallow family. It belongs to the genus Abutilon, which comprises about 150 species of plants, primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions. This particular species is native to the Hawaiian Islands. The classification of Abutilon sandwicense is further detailed in binomial nomenclature, with the species name 'sandwicense' referring to its origin in the Sandwich Islands, which is an old name for Hawaii. This classification helps in the identification and study of the plant's characteristics and distribution.
